guides.text: Let's first understand the goal. $I \to I$ means '$I$ [implication|implies] $I$' — if $I$ is true, then $I$ is true. Hardly controversial.
To prove an [implication|implication], we assume the left-hand side and try to reach the right-hand side. So let's assume $I$.

Now look at line 1. That whole line — $\{I\} \vdash I$ — is called a **[judgement|judgement]**. A judgement is a complete claim: from the stuff on the left, prove the stuff on the right. Every line in the proof area is a judgement.

The $\vdash$ in the middle is the **turnstile** (pronounced '[judgement|entails]'). It splits the judgement: left is the **[context|context]** ($\{I\}$ — the assumptions we can use), right is the **[conclusion|conclusion]** ($I$ — what we're proving at this step).
So $\{I\} \vdash I$ reads: the context $\{I\}$ [judgement|entails] the conclusion $I$. In plain English: assuming $I$, we can prove $I$. Tautological, but logically sound.

Now to finish: '$A$ [implication|implies] $B$' **means** that $B$ follows from assuming $A$. We already have $\{I\} \vdash I$ at step 1. Use `->-intro` to [discharge|discharge] the assumption $I$ and close the proof. Its first argument is the [formula|formula] to [discharge|discharge] and the second is the step number:

unlocks.name: assume
unlocks.description: If you assume a proposition, you can deduce it — because you just said it's true.

unlocks.name: →-intro
unlocks.description: If from assuming $p$ you can reach $q$, then $p$ implies $q$ — and you can safely drop $p$ from the context.
